---
title: "3.5 Methods: How to Write Them"
subtitle: "Class Creation"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Methods: How to Write Them

### Methods provide the behaviors of an object

In object-oriented programming, the textbook keeps three class parts together:

::: {.tight-list}
- instance variables hold the object's data
- constructors initialize that data
- <span class="term">methods</span> define what the object can do
:::

This topic turns class structure into usable behavior.

## Defining and Calling Methods

### Students need to track both definition and use

![Calling methods in different contexts](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-5-methods-how-to-write-them/FlowChartCallingMethods.png){fig-align="center" width="78%"}

The classroom sequence here is:

::: {.tight-list}
- create or obtain an object
- call the method with `objectName.methodName()`
- connect that call to the method header and body inside the class
:::

## `void` and Non-`void`

### Some methods perform actions; others return values

::: {.compare-grid}
::: {.soft-box}
**`void` methods**

- perform an action
- do not return a value
- often print, update, or change state
:::
::: {.soft-box}
**non-`void` methods**

- return one value
- must use a <span class="term">return</span> statement
- include a return type in the header
:::
:::

Code after a guaranteed `return` path does not execute.

## Accessors / Getters

### Getters expose selected data without exposing the variable directly

Because instance variables are usually `private`, outside code needs a public method to read a value.

```java
public String getName()
{
    return name;
}
```

::: {.tight-list}
- a getter usually takes <span class="mark">no parameters</span>
- its return type matches the instance variable type
- it returns a copy of the value, not direct outside access to the variable
:::

## `toString`

### Java uses this method when it needs a string form of an object

`toString` is not exactly a getter, but it is a key non-`void` method.

::: {.tight-list}
- it returns a `String`
- `System.out.println(object)` can call it automatically
- it gives a readable summary of the object's state
:::

This makes it especially useful for tracing and debugging class behavior.

## Mutators / Setters

### A setter changes state through a controlled public method

![Getter and setter comparison](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-5-methods-how-to-write-them/get-set-comparison.png){fig-align="center" width="74%"}

::: {.tight-list}
- a <span class="term">setter</span> is usually a `void` method
- it usually takes one parameter of the same type as the variable being changed
- not every instance variable should automatically get a setter
- the class should expose only the changes that make sense for the object
:::

## Parameters and Arguments

### Java passes copies of argument values into parameter variables

![Arguments become parameters](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-5-methods-how-to-write-them/method-param-arg.png){fig-align="center" width="72%"}

::: {.tight-list}
- a <span class="term">parameter</span> appears in the method header
- an <span class="term">argument</span> is the value used in the method call
- arguments must match the number, order, and compatible types in the signature
- with primitives, changes to the parameter do not change the original argument
:::

## Methods with Parameters and Return Values

### Not every returned value is a simple getter

The textbook keeps one more category:

::: {.tight-list}
- some methods use parameters to perform a search or calculation
- they return a result computed from those inputs
- this combines method parameters, algorithm steps, and a final returned value
:::

This is where method-writing starts to connect back to Unit 2 algorithm work.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

![Animal clinic design prompt](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-5-methods-how-to-write-them/animalclinic.png){fig-align="center" width="46%"}

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- distinguish defining a method from calling a method
- classify methods as `void` or non-`void`
- read and write simple getters, setters, and `toString`
- match arguments to parameters correctly
- analyze methods that compute and return a value
- <span class="term">3.5.9 Coding Challenge: Class Pet</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain the difference between `void` and non-`void` methods
- identify when a `return` statement is required
- describe the purpose of getters, setters, and `toString`
- distinguish parameters from arguments
- trace how a method can both use inputs and return a calculated result
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
